one_line_takeaway: Injecting explicit "my output is harmful" self-suggestion into the GCG optimization target, plus multi-token updates and easy-to-hard suffix reuse, pushes white-box jailbreak ASR to ~100% (including 100% on the hard-to-break Llama2-7B-Chat, up from 54%) while cutting iterations roughly 10x.
key_finding: I-GCG reaches ~100% attack success rate (ASR) on all four white-box victim models. Most notably on the hardest target, Llama2-7B-Chat, it achieves 100% ASR versus GCG 54%, MAC 56%, Probe-Sampling 56%, and AutoDAN 26% (Table 1). On Vicuna-7B-1.5, Guanaco-7B, and Mistral-7B-Instruct-0.2 it also hits 100% (vs e.g. Mistral: AutoDAN 96%, GCG 92%). On the NeurIPS 2023 Red Teaming Track it scores 100% vs ZeroShot 0.1%, GBDA 0.1%, PEZ 0.2% (Table 2). The ablation (Table 4, on Llama2-7B-Chat) shows GCG baseline = 54% ASR / 510 avg iterations; harmful guidance alone = 82% ASR but 955 iterations (raises effectiveness, costs efficiency); update strategy alone = 72% / 418; suffix initialization alone = 68% / 64; all three combined = 100% ASR / 55 iterations. Transferability (suffixes generated on Vicuna, Table 3) remains modest but beats baselines: I-GCG 26%/20%/22%/4% on Mistral/Starling-7B/ChatGPT-3.5/ChatGPT-4 vs GCG 16%/16%/10%/0% and MAC 22%/16%/14%/2%.
core_contribution: The paper introduces I-GCG, a set of three empirical improvements to the Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) optimization-based jailbreak attack (GCG itself is prior work by Zou et al. [55], NOT this paper's contribution). The paper's OWN contributions are: (1) diverse target templates that inject "harmful self-suggestion/guidance" into the optimization goal — instead of GCG's target "Sure, here is + Rephrase(Q)", they optimize toward "Sure, + Harmful Template (e.g. 'my output is harmful'), here is + Rephrase(Q)", arguing the single "Sure" template is insufficient to actually elicit harmful continuation; (2) an automatic multi-coordinate updating strategy that adaptively replaces multiple tokens per iteration (via top-p ranking of single-token candidates and token combination) rather than GCG's single-token-per-step update, to accelerate convergence; (3) an easy-to-hard initialization that first generates a suffix on an easy (fraud-category) malicious question and reuses it to initialize suffixes for harder questions. Combining these yields I-GCG.
threat_model: White-box, optimization/gradient-based jailbreak. The attacker has full access to the victim LLM's weights and gradients (all four primary victims are open-source: Vicuna-7B-1.5, Guanaco-7B, Llama2-7B-Chat, Mistral-7B-Instruct-0.2) and optimizes an adversarial suffix appended to a malicious question to bypass safety alignment and elicit harmful content that would otherwise be refused. A secondary black-box transfer setting is also evaluated, where suffixes optimized on Vicuna are transferred to models the attacker cannot query gradients for (Starling-7B, ChatGPT-3.5, ChatGPT-4), but transfer success is limited (e.g., only 4% on ChatGPT-4). Attacker goal: maximize attack success rate (harmful content elicited) with minimal optimization iterations.
limitations: The authors' stated limitations: transferability to unseen/closed models is still weak (e.g., 4% ASR on ChatGPT-4, 22% on ChatGPT-3.5), and there is room for better harmful-guidance template design and more general suffix initialization; they note transferability-enhancing methods from a cited survey [12] could help. Additional reviewer-observed limits: the attack is fundamentally white-box (needs gradients), so it does not directly threaten closed models except via limited transfer; evaluation uses only 50 AdvBench behaviors and 7B-scale open models; the harmful-guidance mechanism is essentially a hand-designed prompt-engineering trick fused into optimization, and the ASR gains partly depend on the LLM-judge/keyword evaluation pipeline. No defense evaluation (e.g., perplexity filtering, which is known to catch GCG-style gibberish suffixes) is reported.

datasets_used: AdvBench 'harmful behaviors' subset (50 deduplicated representative requests from 520 total)
HarmBench / NeurIPS 2023 Red Teaming Track (Base Model Subtrack)
quantitative_results: I-GCG ASR = 100% on all four white-box victims: Vicuna-7B-1.5, Guanaco-7B, Llama2-7B-Chat, Mistral-7B-Instruct-0.2 (Table 1)
Llama2-7B-Chat: I-GCG 100% vs GCG 54%, MAC 56%, Probe-Sampling 56%, AutoDAN 26%, PAIR 10%, TAP 4%, AmpleGCG 28%, AdvPrompter 24% (Table 1)
NeurIPS 2023 Red Teaming Track: I-GCG ~100% vs ZeroShot 0.1%, GBDA 0.1%, PEZ 0.2% (Table 2)
Ablation on Llama2-7B-Chat (Table 4): baseline GCG 54% ASR/510 iters; +harmful guidance 82%/955; +update strategy 72%/418; +suffix init 68%/64; all combined 100%/55
Transferability from Vicuna-7B-1.5 (Table 3): I-GCG 26%/20%/22%/4% on Mistral/Starling-7B-Alpha/ChatGPT-3.5/ChatGPT-4 vs GCG 16%/16%/10%/0%, MAC 22%/16%/14%/2%
Hyperparameter p=7 converges in ~400 steps vs original GCG ~2000 steps (Fig. 7)
Table 5: five different harmful-guidance phrasings all reach 100% ASR on Llama2-7B-Chat
